title: Dear Ijeawele, or A Feminist Manifesto in Fifteen Suggestions

chapters.title: We must build a fair world for women and men
chapters.text:
It can be hard to believe that this feminist manifesto began as a private letter from one woman to a friend. A simple note between two friends has become a guide for many readers around the world.

When Ms. Adichie was first asked by a friend to write down advice on raising a feminist daughter, she froze. The task felt too big. How can anyone hand over advice that helps shape a child's character. She accepted the challenge anyway and poured her thoughts into the manifesto you are about to read.

As Adichie has written, she decided to write a letter that was honest and practical, a kind of map of her own feminist thinking. Now that she has a daughter of her own, the advice carries even more weight. She feels a stronger drive to change the world around her. We have to talk about raising children in a new way, because that is the only path to real equality.

Raising a feminist child, or living as a feminist yourself, is not easy. There is no single rulebook, since feminism is always shaped by context. You do, however, need a core belief to start from. Your starting point should be the idea that you matter completely, always. Not matter as long as, not matter because of, not matter despite. You matter, full stop. Even though feminism is contextual, its main point is that women matter equally. End of story.

chapters.title: Dropping gender roles frees us to be ourselves
chapters.text:
Parenting and motherhood are beautiful gifts, but they should never erase who you are. Losing yourself in your child hurts both of you over time. If you love your work, do not feel guilty for combining motherhood and a career. Going against the old script does not make you a bad mother. It makes you a powerful example for your children. Combining motherhood and work is not a betrayal of tradition. It is a way to make the whole family stronger.

As Adichie has pointed out, people will pick and choose which traditions to defend. Every part of raising a child should be shared by both parents. A father should do everything that biology allows, which is just about everything except breastfeeding.

Gender roles have placed the full weight of childcare on women, and that idea needs to end. Childcare should be split fairly, with each person's needs in mind. We also need to retire the word help. When a father cares for his child, he is not helping or babysitting. He is simply doing his job. Neither gender deserves a medal for looking after their own children. They are meeting a basic responsibility.

Childcare should be split equally, and a woman should not feel like a single mother unless she really is one. The next step is to drop gender roles entirely, because they are nonsense. From birth, we are taught to like certain things and act in certain ways because of our gender. Children are often forced to hide their true selves to fit a mold. Parents should never tell a child what to do or not do simply because of gender.

As Adichie has said, because you are a girl is never a reason for anything. Ever. Gender roles show up in small daily moments. Toy aisles are a clear example. Boy toys are often active, like cars, trains, and planes. Girl toys are often passive, like dolls and kitchen sets. These tiny details shape young minds and lock in the larger social pattern.

When we stop forcing children into gender roles, we open up space for everyone to reach their full potential.

chapters.title: Language has the power to shape who we become
chapters.text:
Feminism is a lot like pregnancy. You either are, or you are not. There is no in-between. You either believe in full equality for men and women, or you do not. The danger lives in the in-between, which Adichie calls Feminism Lite.

If you have heard phrases like a man is the head, but a woman is the neck, or a man drives, but a woman rides in the front seat, you know exactly what Feminism Lite looks like. It pushes the dangerous idea that men are somehow above women. It leans on the language of allowing. The word allow is risky, because it implies that one person holds power over another, and that power usually sits with men. So when you raise a child, you have to keep Feminism Lite out of the house.

Feminism Lite feeds the idea of male superiority and erases the idea of a strong woman. Two of the suggestions in the manifesto go hand in hand. First, teach your daughters and your sons to love books. Second, respect the power of language.

The best way to grow a reader is to read yourself. When your child sees you reading, she will learn that books are a valuable, exciting pastime. Reading will help her question the world, sometimes even better than any formal class.

Books help a child express herself, and they help her in any future path, from chef to scientist to singer. As Adichie has written, books will help her express herself and help her in whatever she wants to become, since every craft benefits from strong reading skills.

Respecting language also means choosing your words with care. A girl who grows up hearing she is a princess turns out different from a girl who is often called an angel. The words you use teach your child what to value.

chapters.title: Being yourself matters more than being liked
chapters.text:
Part of raising a feminist daughter is changing the meaning of marriage. For centuries, women were taught to treat marriage as the finish line. That is not a healthy target for a young girl, and boys are rarely raised that way. The imbalance starts early.

As Adichie has said, a marriage can be happy or unhappy, but that is not an achievement. Teach your daughter to treat marriage as an equal partnership between two people. That mindset will help her, one day, build a healthy relationship.

Another big idea is the rejection of likeability. Our society often teaches girls to be likeable, nice, and therefore false. That pressure smothers authenticity, and it can have serious consequences. Predators count on a girl who has been trained to be quiet and polite.

As Adichie has written, we have a world full of women who are unable to exhale fully, because they have for so long been trained to fold themselves into shapes that make them likeable. Women raised on the ideas of likeability and niceness rarely grow into their full selves. We have to push back on that pattern and raise a generation of girls who know how to be honest.

Instead of teaching your daughter to be likeable, teach her to be honest and kind, in that order.

chapters.title: We can rewrite any social norm we want
chapters.text:
How a person, especially a woman, sees her own appearance can shape her self-worth. You have to be careful about how you talk about looks with your daughter.

Encourage her to play sports. Movement is good for health and for body image. Let her know that being active is valuable. Do not lock her into a single style of fashion or makeup. If she wants to be feminine, that is fine. If she rejects traditional femininity, that is also fine. Feminism and femininity are not enemies. They can and should live side by side.

Never tie a woman's appearance to her morality. Clothing is a question of taste, not a question of virtue. Teach your daughter to question the so-called biological basis of social norms, because those norms are biased and selective. We use biology to defend male privilege but rarely grant the same natural rights to women. For example, children often get their father's last name, patronymic, or religion, even though the mother is the more obvious biological link. Why are children identified with their fathers instead of their mothers. Biology would suggest the opposite, but the rule is set in man's favor.

Most social norms are human-made, which means none of them are set in stone.

chapters.title: We have to remove the shame around female sexuality
chapters.text:
No matter how awkward it feels, you have to talk with your daughter about sex. The talk should be free of judgment, completely honest, and grounded in reality. Share the beauty of sex, and the responsibilities that come with it. The most important part is to make sure your daughter feels safe raising the topic with you at any time. Help her understand that her body belongs to her, and nothing changes that.

Saying no in the right moment and owning your body is something to be proud of. Make sure your daughter does not inherit the shame that society attaches to female sexuality, and the first step is to let go of that shame yourself.

Love is a beautiful thing, so be ready to support your daughter's romantic life. Together with your partner, model a healthy relationship that does not lean on old stereotypes. Teach her that love is a two-way street of giving and receiving.

Raising daughters as feminists can open the door to a new kind of relationship, one where the roles are not set in advance. Whoever can give, gives. Either person can propose, and the question of marriage becomes a calm, joyful conversation rather than a charged ritual.

As Adichie has written, she truly wishes for a world where either person can propose, where a relationship has become so comfortable and full of joy that the choice to marry is itself a happy talk.

chapters.title: Conclusion: build your own feminist manifesto
chapters.text:
Raising your daughter, or yourself, as a feminist is not easy, but it pays off in the long run. As you follow the suggestions in this manifesto, do not forget that our world is built on differences. Teaching children about those differences helps them move through a diverse world with confidence.

Feminism is not a universal rulebook. You should not push your standards and experiences on everyone else, since they fit your own life. The suggestions here are not universal truths either. They are a framework and a starting point. In the end, when you raise a child, trust your instincts above all, since instincts are guided by the purest form of love.

Choose the ideas that matter most to you and build your own feminist manifesto around them. Talk with the people you care about and share what women face every day. Lead by example, and let your actions teach the next generation.
